common_name: Candlestick Senna (Empress Candle, Ringworm Bush, Seven Golden Candlesticks)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost; in zone 9 plant March–May for late summer and fall bloom. Seeds have a hard coat — nick or scarify and soak overnight in warm water for best germination. In zone 9, Candlestick Senna grows as a fast-growing annual or short-lived perennial, dying to the ground in frost but often reseeding prolifically. It self-seeds to the point of becoming weedy in zone 10-11 — deadhead spent spikes before seeds mature if spreading is a concern.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile soil; Candlestick Senna grows rapidly — a spring-planted seedling often reaches 6–8 feet and begins blooming by August–September in zone 9. Senna alata (Candlestick Senna or Empress Candle) is a stunning tropical shrub or sub-shrub native to tropical America and now naturalized throughout the world's tropics, producing one of the most dramatic flower displays in the subtropical garden: erect, rigid, conical flower spikes 12–18 inches long closely packed with bright golden-yellow individual flowers that open progressively from the base upward — the unopened buds at the tip are enclosed in overlapping orange-brown bracts that suggest golden candle flames, inspiring the common name. Multiple spikes arise simultaneously from each stem tip in August–November in zone 9, creating an extraordinary golden spectacle when the plant is in full bloom. The large, pinnately compound leaves are architectural and attractive even out of bloom. Senna alata is also famous for its potent antifungal properties — leaf extracts have been shown effective against Microsporum canis, Trichophyton species, and other dermatophytes responsible for ringworm and tinea infections, hence the folk medicine name 'Ringworm Bush.' An outstanding butterfly plant as well — Orange-barred Sulfur and Cloudless Sulphur butterflies use it as a larval host plant throughout the Gulf Coast.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Senna alata (Candlestick Senna)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Senna alata
USDA Plants Database — Senna alata
Journal of Ethnopharmacology — Antifungal properties of Senna alata leaf extracts

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring when soil temperature reaches 70°F in partial shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil; Beehive Ginger prefers the same conditions as culinary ginger — warm, humid, partially shaded, with good drainage. In zone 9, the rhizomes survive mild winters underground but may die in hard freezes; mulch heavily in zone 9a. Divide clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor. The spectacular inflorescences last 4–6 weeks on the plant and 3–4 weeks as cut flowers.

directions: Plant rhizomes 2 inches deep in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Beehive Ginger performs best in morning sun with afternoon shade in zone 9's intense summer heat. Zingiber spectabile is a large ornamental ginger from Malaysia and Borneo whose primary attraction is its extraordinary inflorescence — a dense, upright, waxy, beehive-shaped structure of tightly overlapping bracts that emerges at soil level on a short stem. The bracts begin cream-white to pale yellow and age through rich golden-yellow to deep orange and finally red as the inflorescence matures, eventually resembling a burning torch. Individual yellow flowers with purple markings emerge from between the bracts. The inflorescence is exceptionally long-lasting — months on the plant — and makes spectacular cut flowers or dried specimens that retain color for years. The plant produces large, dramatic clumps of tall canes with strap-like leaves similar to culinary ginger but much more ornamental. Beehive Ginger is one of the most exotic and conversation-stopping ornamental gingers for zone 9 shade gardens and tropical plantings; its unusual flower form and long season of interest make it a prized specimen in botanical gardens worldwide. The rhizomes are not edible (unlike Zingiber officinale) but have no reported toxicity.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental Gingers for Florida
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Zingiber spectabile cultivation
International Society for Horticultural Science — Ornamental Ginger Genera
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Flowering Plants for Louisiana Landscapes

common_name: Cockspur Coral Tree (Ceibo, Cry Baby Tree, Fireman's Cap)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after last frost; in zone 9, Cockspur Coral Tree dies back to the ground or trunk in cold winters but resprouts vigorously each spring and often blooms in the same year after being cut back. In zone 10-11 it is an evergreen or semi-deciduous tree; in zone 9 it behaves as a large die-back perennial. The thorny stems require care — wear heavy gloves when pruning. Seeds are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; Cockspur Coral Tree is quite drought-tolerant once established and tolerates a wide range of soils including sand and alkaline conditions. Erythrina crista-galli (Cockspur Coral Tree or Ceibo) is the national tree and national flower of both Argentina and Uruguay — its spectacular, intense red flowers are also depicted in the coat of arms of Argentina. The flowers are large (2–2.5 inches), waxy, brilliant red, and clustered into drooping racemes at the branch tips in spring and summer; the characteristic 'crista-galli' (rooster's crest) refers to the hooded shape of the large standard petal. In warm climates the tree blooms March–June and again in fall, attracting hummingbirds, orioles, and numerous other nectar-feeding birds. In zone 9, the plant dies back to the root system after hard frost each winter and regrows to 5–8 feet by the following summer, blooming on new wood — acting effectively as a large, spectacular die-back perennial. In zone 10-11 it grows into a 30-foot tree with massive flowering displays. The thorny branches and trunks are a defensive characteristic; all parts of the plant are toxic if consumed. 'Compacta' is a dwarf cultivar reaching 6–8 feet suitable for smaller gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Erythrina crista-galli (Cockspur Coral Tree)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Erythrina species for Gulf Coast
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Erythrina crista-galli species profile
Argentine National Research Council (CONICET) — Ceibo national flower heritage

planting.note: Plant as a container specimen in zone 9 — Madagascar Dragon Tree is damaged by temperatures below 35°F and cannot be safely planted outdoors in zone 9a year-round. Bring containers indoors or to a frost-free space when temperatures drop below 40°F. In zone 10-11, plant outdoors in partial shade with well-drained soil. Excellent for patios and atriums as a year-round container plant that goes outdoors in warm weather.

directions: Plant in partial shade in well-drained soil or potting mix; Madagascar Dragon Tree adapts to low-light conditions better than most tropical plants, making it one of the premier houseplants and patio container specimens. Dracaena marginata (Madagascar Dragon Tree) is native to Madagascar and Mauritius and has become one of the most popular large houseplants and outdoor landscape specimens in subtropical and tropical regions worldwide. The narrow, red-edged, strap-like leaves radiate in rosettes from the tips of slender, branching stems that develop a distinctive bottle-palm-like silhouette as they age. Multiple color forms are available: the standard 'Marginata' (red-edged green leaves), 'Tricolor' (green, cream, and red stripes), 'Colorama' (dark red-purple throughout), and 'Tarzan' (wide, arching leaves). Dracaena marginata is exceptionally forgiving of neglect — it tolerates low light, infrequent watering, dry indoor air, and occasional drought better than almost any other large tropical plant. Plants that get regular outdoor exposure to bright indirect light and occasional fertilization in the growing season grow substantially faster and maintain better color than those kept exclusively indoors. In zone 10-11 it can be planted outdoors as a landscape specimen reaching 15–20 feet with multiple branching trunks. NASA Clean Air Study data suggest it removes formaldehyde, benzene, and trichloroethylene from indoor air.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dracaena marginata for landscape use
Royal Horticultural Society — Dracaena marginata cultivation
NASA Clean Air Study (1989) — Interior foliage plants for air cleaning
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Dracaena marginata

common_name: Corn Plant Dracaena (Mass Cane, Fortune Plant)

planting.note: Grow as a container specimen in zone 9 and bring indoors when temperatures fall below 40°F; Corn Plant Dracaena is damaged by cold and should not be left outdoors in zone 9a winters. In zone 10-11 it can be planted in the ground in partial shade. The plant is highly toxic to dogs and cats — keep away from pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to low light in well-drained potting mix in containers, or in zone 10-11 landscape shade; Corn Plant Dracaena is one of the most low-light tolerant large foliage plants available and can be maintained in surprisingly dim indoor conditions that would kill virtually any other large houseplant. Dracaena fragrans is native to tropical Africa and has been in cultivation as a houseplant for over 150 years. The most popular cultivar, 'Massangeana' (Mass Cane), features broad, arching, corn-like leaves with a wide yellow stripe down the center — the combination of bold form and golden-yellow variegation makes it one of the most striking large foliage plants for interiors and shaded patios. The thick, woody cane-like stems can be 'cane cut' and new growth will sprout from latent buds — a common propagation technique that also allows the plant to be maintained at a desired height. In zone 10-11 landscapes, the plant grows into a multi-trunked small tree 15–25 feet tall and occasionally produces fragrant white flowers at night. Corn Plant Dracaena removes benzene, formaldehyde, trichloroethylene, xylene, and toluene from indoor air according to the NASA Clean Air Study. CAUTION: Toxic to dogs and cats — causes vomiting, dilated pupils, and weakness; keep away from pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dracaena fragrans (Corn Plant)
NASA Clean Air Study (1989) — B.C. Wolverton — Interior plants for air quality
ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center — Dracaena species toxicity
Royal Horticultural Society — Dracaena fragrans cultivation notes
